name: Brute Force (every pair)
summary: Compare every pair of elements; if any two are equal, there is a duplicate. No extra memory, but O(n²) comparisons.
visualizationIntent: init: the array of values is shown. loop: pointer i fixes an anchor element (special). move: pointer j sweeps every later element (current). check: compare nums[i] and nums[j]; a readout shows the two values. done: when nums[i] == nums[j], both cells glow result (green) — a duplicate pair is found, return true. return: if no pair ever matched, return false.

lineExplanations.3: Record the number of elements so both loops know where to stop.
lineExplanations.4: Fix an anchor element at index i.
lineExplanations.5: Pick a later element at index j to compare against the anchor.
lineExplanations.6: Check whether the anchor and this element are equal.
lineExplanations.7: They are equal — a duplicate pair exists, return true.
lineExplanations.8: No pair matched anywhere, so return false.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: — the class wrapper the platform expects your method to live inside.
syntaxExplanations.2: def containsDuplicate(self, nums): — defines the method receiving the integer array.
syntaxExplanations.3: n = len(nums) — the number of elements, used to bound both loops.
syntaxExplanations.4: for i in range(n): — outer loop picks the anchor index i.
syntaxExplanations.5: for j in range(i + 1, n): — inner loop picks a later index j (so each pair is tested once).
syntaxExplanations.6: if nums[i] == nums[j]: — are these two elements equal?
syntaxExplanations.7: return True — equal elements found, so a duplicate exists.
syntaxExplanations.8: return False — no equal pair anywhere, so all elements are distinct.
